agent_output: {"executive_summary":"This synchronous-heavy order processing architecture has multiple catastrophic failure modes stemming from tight temporal coupling between Order Service, Payment Service, and Stripe. The fire-and-forget notification path silently loses emails. The design needs circuit breakers, idempotency keys, transactional outbox, and a saga/compensation pattern to achieve production-grade resilience.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts customer orders, orchestrates payment and notification. Currently the synchronous orchestrator — if it crashes mid-flow, state is lost.","boundaries":"Owns order lifecycle (created → paid → confirmed → notified). Should NOT own payment or notification state."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Abstracts payment gateway interactions. Translates internal payment requests to Stripe API calls.","boundaries":"Owns payment attempt records, idempotency keys, and retry logic against Stripe. Should be the sole Stripe integration point."},{"name":"Stripe (Third-Party Gateway)","responsibility":"Processes actual charges. External dependency outside our control.","boundaries":"External system. We cannot control its availability, latency, or error semantics. Must be treated as fundamentally unreliable."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends confirmation emails to customers after successful order placement.","boundaries":"Owns email delivery. Currently receives fire-and-forget HTTP POSTs — no delivery guarantee."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP POST /payments with order ID, amount, currency, idempotency key. Returns payment status.","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral (Order blocks on Payment response; Order's success depends on Payment's success)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Stripe","contract":"Synchronous HTTPS to Stripe Charges API. External contract we conform to.","coupling_type":"temporal + platform (blocks on external network call; subject to Stripe's rate limits, outages, and API changes)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Fire-and-forget HTTP POST /notifications with order details. No response verification.","coupling_type":"fire-and-forget (no delivery guarantee, no retry, no acknowledgment)"}],"data_flow":"Customer → Order Service (sync) → Payment Service (sync) → Stripe (sync) → Payment Service → Order Service → Notification Service (async fire-and-forget). The entire payment path is a synchronous chain where any link failing cascades to the caller.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Prevents cascading failure when Payment Service or Stripe is down. Order Service stops hammering a dead downstream and fails fast, preserving its own thread pool and latency SLA."},{"pattern":"Idempotency Keys","rationale":"Network partitions and retries can cause duplicate charges. Every payment request must carry a client-generated idempotency key so Stripe and Payment Service can deduplicate safely."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Replaces fire-and-forget notification with guaranteed-delivery. Order Service writes a notification event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the order state change. A relay process publishes it to a message broker or polls and sends."},{"pattern":"Retry with Exponential Backoff + Jitter","rationale":"Transient failures (TCP resets, 503s, Stripe rate limits) are recoverable if retried with backoff. Jitter prevents thundering herd on recovery."},{"pattern":"Timeout + Deadline Propagation","rationale":"Without explicit timeouts, a hung Stripe call blocks the Payment Service thread, which blocks the Order Service thread, which blocks the customer. Deadlines propagate remaining budget through the call chain."},{"pattern":"Saga / Compensating Transaction","rationale":"If payment succeeds but Order Service crashes before persisting the result, we need a reconciliation mechanism. A saga pattern ensures either the order is marked paid or the charge is refunded."},{"pattern":"Bulkhead","rationale":"Isolate thread pools for payment calls vs other Order Service operations so a Stripe slowdown doesn't starve unrelated order queries."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, states: created/payment_pending/paid/confirmed/failed, invariant: cannot confirm unpaid order)"]},{"name":"Payment Processing","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentAttempt (root: PaymentAttemptId, tracks idempotency key, gateway reference, status, amount)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: NotificationId, tracks delivery status, retry count, channel)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Payment Processing","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Payment Processing defines payment result schema; Order Management conforms to it but can request changes."},{"upstream":"Stripe","downstream":"Payment Processing","relationship":"Conformist with Anti-Corruption Layer — We conform to Stripe's API but wrap it in an ACL to translate Stripe-specific errors, webhooks, and models into our domain language."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via Transactional Outbox — Order Management publishes domain events; Notification subscribes. Notification never dictates order model."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment as synchronous HTTP with circuit breaker rather than going fully async","gains":"Simpler mental model — customer gets immediate payment confirmation. Fewer moving parts than a full async saga. Easier to reason about in debugging.","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is still coupled to Payment Service availability (mitigated by circuit breaker). Peak throughput limited by synchronous call latency.","alternatives_considered":["Fully async payment via message queue with callback — higher resilience but adds complexity of pending payment states, webhook reconciliation, and customer-facing polling/push","Payment processed entirely via Stripe webhooks — eliminates sync call but introduces webhook reliability concerns and delayed confirmation UX"]},{"decision":"Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox + message broker","gains":"Zero notification loss. At-least-once delivery guarantee. Notification failures no longer silent. Decouples notification processing from order latency.","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure (outbox table, relay/CDC process, message broker). Slightly more complex deployment. Notifications become eventually consistent (delay possible).","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous notification call with retry — adds latency to order flow, customer waits for email send","Keep fire-and-forget but add a reconciliation job — cheaper but notifications lost between job runs"]},{"decision":"Idempotency keys generated by Order Service, not Payment Service","gains":"True end-to-end idempotency. Safe to retry the entire Order→Payment flow without double-charging. Stripe natively supports idempotency keys.","sacrifices":"Order Service must persist the idempotency key before calling Payment Service, adding a DB write to the critical path.","alternatives_considered":["Payment Service generates its own idempotency key — doesn't protect against Order Service retrying with a new Payment Service request","Database-level dedup in Payment Service — works but is fragile and Stripe-unaware"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment Gateway","evolution_path":"The ACL around Stripe means swapping to Adyen, Braintree, or adding multi-gateway routing is isolated to the Payment Service internals. No Order Service changes needed."},{"area":"Notification Channel","evolution_path":"Transactional outbox publishes domain events (OrderConfirmed). Notification Service can evolve to support SMS, push, webhooks without changing the upstream contract."},{"area":"Sync-to-Async Payment Migration","evolution_path":"Circuit breaker + idempotency keys are prerequisites for async payment. If load demands it, the sync call can be replaced with a queue + webhook pattern without re-architecting idempotency or failure handling."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order→Payment synchronous coupling","reason":"Intentionally rigid. Customers expect immediate payment confirmation. Making this async changes the UX contract and requires frontend polling infrastructure. Keep sync until scale forces the change."},{"area":"Idempotency key format and generation","reason":"Once idempotency keys are in production and stored in Stripe, changing the format or generation strategy requires careful migration. Lock this down early."},{"area":"Order state machine transitions","reason":"The order lifecycle (created → payment_pending → paid → confirmed → failed) is a core invariant. Adding or reordering states has wide impact. Design it carefully upfront."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Stripe outage causes complete order processing failure","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Circuit breaker with fallback (queue for retry, or graceful degradation UI telling customer payment is being processed). Payment reconciliation job runs on recovery. Stripe status page integration for proactive alerting."},{"risk":"Partial failure: Stripe charges card but Payment Service crashes before returning success to Order Service","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Idempotency keys allow safe retry. Payment reconciliation job compares Stripe charges against local PaymentAttempt records. Saga compensation refunds orphaned charges."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss (current fire-and-forget design)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Transactional outbox guarantees at-least-once delivery. Dead letter queue for notifications that fail after max retries. Monitoring on outbox lag and DLQ depth."},{"risk":"Cascading latency: Stripe slowdown (e.g., 10s response times) exhausts Order Service thread pool","severity":"high","mitigation":"Bulkhead isolates payment threads. Aggressive timeouts (e.g., 5s for Stripe call, 8s for end-to-end). Circuit breaker opens on latency threshold, not just errors."},{"risk":"Retry storm after Stripe recovery causes thundering herd","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Exponential backoff with jitter on all retry paths. Circuit breaker half-open state limits probe rate. Rate limiting on Payment Service outbound calls."},{"risk":"Idempotency key collision or reuse across different orders","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Key format: order_id + attempt_number (deterministic, unique per order). Validate uniqueness in Payment Service before forwarding to Stripe."},{"risk":"Outbox relay falls behind under high load, causing notification delays","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Monitor outbox table size and relay lag. Scale relay workers horizontally. Set alerting threshold for acceptable delay (e.g., 60s)."},{"risk":"Stripe API version deprecation breaks Payment Service","severity":"low","mitigation":"ACL isolates Stripe-specific logic. Pin Stripe API version in requests. Monitor Stripe deprecation notices. Run integration tests against Stripe test mode in CI."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Add timeouts and circuit breaker to Order→Payment call. Add idempotency keys (Order Service generates, Payment Service forwards to Stripe). These two changes eliminate the two critical risks with minimal infrastructure. Phase 2: Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox pattern. Phase 3: Add bulkhead isolation, payment reconciliation job, and Stripe webhook listener for belt-and-suspenders confirmation. Phase 4: Observability — distributed tracing across all three services, circuit breaker state dashboards, outbox lag metrics.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/payment-client.{ext} — Add circuit breaker (e.g., resilience4j, polly, opossum), timeout config, retry with backoff","order-service/src/services/order-service.{ext} — Generate idempotency key before payment call, persist to order record","order-service/src/models/outbox-event.{ext} — Outbox table entity for guaranteed notification delivery","order-service/src/workers/outbox-relay.{ext} — Polls outbox table, publishes to message broker, marks as sent","payment-service/src/clients/stripe-client.{ext} — ACL wrapper around Stripe SDK, forwards idempotency key, translates errors","payment-service/src/services/payment-service.{ext} — Persist PaymentAttempt before calling Stripe, update on response","payment-service/src/jobs/reconciliation-job.{ext} — Compares local payment records against Stripe charges, flags discrepancies","notification-service/src/consumers/order-confirmed-consumer.{ext} — Subscribes to OrderConfirmed events from broker instead of receiving HTTP POSTs"],"patterns_to_follow":"Circuit Breaker: Use a proven library (resilience4j for Java, polly for .NET, opossum for Node). Do not hand-roll. Configure: failure threshold 50%, wait duration 30s, half-open permits 3. Idempotency: Key = SHA256(orderId + attemptNumber), sent as Idempotency-Key header to Stripe. Outbox: Same-transaction write pattern — INSERT INTO outbox in the same DB transaction as the order state change. Relay deletes after confirmed publish. Timeouts: Stripe call 5s, Payment Service total 8s, Order Service total 12s. Each layer sheds 2-3s for its own processing.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT retry without idempotency keys — this is how double-charges happen","Do NOT use in-memory circuit breaker state in a multi-instance deployment without shared state or per-instance breakers with identical config","Do NOT catch and swallow payment errors — every failure must be logged with correlation ID and surfaced to the order state machine","Do NOT add a message broker for notifications without the outbox pattern — publishing to the broker outside the DB transaction means you can commit the order but fail to publish, losing the notification anyway","Do NOT set Stripe timeouts higher than your own API timeout — the customer gets a gateway timeout while Stripe is still processing","Do NOT rely solely on Stripe webhooks for payment confirmation — webhooks can be delayed or lost. Use them as secondary confirmation, not primary."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-known distributed systems failure patterns","Circuit breaker and timeout recommendations — industry standard","Idempotency key design — Stripe natively supports this pattern","Transactional outbox replacing fire-and-forget — proven pattern for exactly this problem"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific timeout values (5s/8s/12s) — these should be tuned based on observed P99 latencies in the actual deployment","Circuit breaker thresholds — 50%/30s are reasonable defaults but need tuning under real traffic patterns","Whether the team's infrastructure already includes a message broker — outbox pattern needs one"],"assumptions":["Services are stateless and horizontally scaled behind a load balancer","Each service has its own database (no shared DB)","Stripe is the sole payment gateway (no multi-gateway routing needed today)","Email is the only notification channel currently","The team has operational maturity to run a message broker (RabbitMQ, Kafka, SQS, etc.)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"This synchronous-heavy order processing architecture has multiple catastrophic failure modes stemming from tight temporal coupling between Order Service, Payment Service, and Stripe. The fire-and-forget notification path silently loses emails. The design needs circuit breakers, idempotency keys, transactional outbox, and a saga/compensation pattern to achieve production-grade resilience.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts custom
